title: Chronic Kidney Disease
oneLiner: Progressive loss of kidney function (eGFR <60 mL/min for >3 months) – common, silent, high CV risk.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hyperkalaemia:**
  - Single finding: ECG changes – peaked T waves, loss of P wave, widened QRS (call this "tented T" or "sine wave" – do not wait for K+ result if ECG is abnormal)
- **Pulmonary oedema / fluid overload:**
  - Single finding: Bilateral crackles up to lung apices + orthopnoea + raised JVP – patient cannot lie flat
- **Uraemic pericarditis / tamponade:**
  - Single finding: Chest pain + pericardial rub + hypotension + pulsus paradoxus on examination
sections.Presentation:
- Asymptomatic in early stages (incidental finding on bloods)
- Later: fatigue, pruritus, anorexia, nausea, weight loss, restless legs, peripheral oedema, dyspnoea, confusion, hiccups
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hypertension (often resistant)
  - Pallor, bruising, scratch marks
  - Peripheral oedema (pitting), pleural effusion, ascites
  - Asterixis (flapping tremor) if uraemic
  - Foetor uraemicus (urine-like breath)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute kidney injury (AKI):** acute rise in creatinine (hours–days), often normal or large kidneys on ultrasound, no anaemia / mineral bone disease (unless pre-existing CKD)
- **Pre-renal AKI:** history of vomiting/diarrhoea, dehydration, low urine sodium (<20 mmol/L), rapid response to fluids
- **Obstructive uropathy:** acute or chronic – pain, anuria, palpable bladder; USS shows hydronephrosis
- **Drug-induced renal impairment:** NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, contrast, aminoglycosides – check drug history
- **Decompensated chronic heart failure:** mimics CKD fluid overload but BNP raised, echo shows systolic/diastolic dysfunction
- **Splitter:** Renal ultrasound showing **small, echogenic kidneys** (≤9 cm) strongly favours CKD over AKI; normal or large kidneys with normal echogenicity suggests AKI (or polycystic kidney disease)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine dipstick – protein, blood, leucocytes, nitrites, glucose
  - ECG – assess for hyperkalaemia (tented T, wide QRS)
  - Fluid balance chart + daily weight
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine, eGFR (using CKD-EPI equation)
  - FBC (normocytic anaemia), bone profile (Ca, PO4, ALP), bicarbonate, albumin
  - PTH, vitamin D (if G4–G5)
  - Serum bicarbonate (assess metabolic acidosis)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Renal ultrasound** (size, echogenicity, cortical thickness, hydronephrosis, stones)
  - Second-line: CT KUB without contrast if obstruction suspected but not confirmed on USS
- **Special tests:**
  - Urine protein:creatinine ratio (PCR) or albumin:creatinine ratio (ACR) – quantify proteinuria (A1: <3, A2: 3–30, A3: >30 mg/mmol)
  - Serology if suspected glomerulonephritis: ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, complement C3/C4, hepatitis B/C, HIV
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **KDIGO / NICE classification:**
    - eGFR G1: ≥90; G2: 60–89; G3a: 45–59; G3b: 30–44; G4: 15–29; G5: <15
    - Combine with ACR category (A1/A2/A3) – higher ACR = worse prognosis
  - Diagnosis requires eGFR <60 or ACR >3 for >3 months (with or without structural damage)
- **Never-miss test:** **Renal ultrasound (or CT) to exclude obstruction** – omission can lead to irreversible renal loss from treatable hydronephrosis
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.5 or >6.0 with ECG changes):**
    - IV calcium gluconate 10 mL 10% over 5–10 min (cardioprotection)
    - IV insulin 10 units Actrapid + 50 mL 50% glucose (shift K+ intracellularly)
    - Consider salbutamol nebulised 5–10 mg (adjunct)
    - Arrange review for dialysis if refractory
  - **Pulmonary oedema:**
    - Sit patient upright, high-flow O2, IV furosemide 40–80 mg (if not anuric)
    - Consider IV nitrates (GTN 10–200 mcg/min) for preload reduction
    - If no response – urgent nephrology review + dialysis (usually haemodialysis)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **Stop nephrotoxic drugs:** NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB temporarily if AKI on CKD, metformin (if eGFR <30), gentamicin (avoid unless essential)
  - **Fluid management:** restrict if oedema (1–1.5 L/day); replace obligate losses if dehydrated
  - **Metabolic acidosis:** if HCO3 <22, consider oral sodium bicarbonate 500 mg–1 g tds [check local policy]
  - **Anaemia:** check iron, folate, B12; start oral iron if ferritin low (ferrous sulphate 200 mg tds); if persistent with eGFR <30, consider ESA (e.g., epoetin alfa – dose per local protocol)
  - **CKD-mineral bone disorder:** check PO4, Ca, PTH; if PO4 >1.5 mmol/L, start phosphate binder (e.g., calcium carbonate 500 mg–1 g with meals); vitamin D – prescribe colecalciferol if deficient or alfacalcidol for active vitamin D [check local policy]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **BP target:** <130/80 mmHg (or <125/75 if ACR >70 mg/mmol) – use ACEi/ARB as first-line (see Drugs)
  - **Diabetes management:** optimise HbA1c <58 mmol/mol; adjust insulin doses if eGFR <30 (reduced clearance)
  - **Lifestyle:** smoking cessation, low-salt diet (<6 g/day), exercise, maintain BMI 20–25
  - **Vaccinations:** seasonal flu, pneumococcal, hepatitis B (if planning RRT)
  - **Referral to nephrology:** eGFR <30 (G4–G5), rapid decline (>5 mL/min/year), ACR >70 mg/mmol, or resistant hypertension
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV fluids only if hypovolaemic – use 0.9% saline (avoid Hartmann’s if K+ high); restrict to 1–1.5 L/day IV/PO if fluid overloaded
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH at standard doses – check anti-Xa if eGFR <30 (use enoxaparin 20 mg daily or tinzaparin 3500 units daily) [check local policy]
  - **Diet:** normal unless fluid overloaded (then 1–1.5 L fluid restriction; if hyperkalaemic – low-K+ diet)
  - **Observation:** 4–6 hourly NEWS, daily standing weight, strict fluid balance, daily U&E
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **ACEi (ramipril):** start 1.25 mg od, titrate to 2.5–10 mg od – reduce dose if eGFR <30, check K+ and creatinine 1–2 weeks after each dose increase. **ARB (losartan):** 25–50 mg od, same monitoring.
- **Furosemide:** 40–80 mg IV/PO, max 250 mg IV over 4 h (if oliguric). Monitor K+, Mg.
- **Statin (atorvastatin):** 20 mg od for primary CV prevention; 40–80 mg od for secondary (no dose adjustment in CKD)
- **Anaemia:**
  - Oral iron: ferrous sulphate 200 mg tds
  - IV iron: e.g., ferric carboxymaltose 500–1000 mg as per local protocol
  - Epoetin alfa: [check local policy] – typically 2000–4000 IU SC weekly
- **Phosphate binders:** calcium carbonate 500–1000 mg with meals; sevelamer (if hypercalcaemia) 800–1600 mg tds
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **NSAIDs** – absolute contraindication in CKD (increase risk of AKI, worsen proteinuria)
  - **Metformin** – stop if eGFR <30 (risk of lactic acidosis)
  - **Gentamicin** – avoid unless no alternative; use once-daily dosing + monitor levels (trough <1 mg/L)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting/upscaling **ACEi or ARB without checking K+ and creatinine** within 1–2 weeks – high risk of hyperkalaemia or acute decline in eGFR
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hyperkalaemia (life-threatening arrhythmia)
  - Pulmonary oedema
  - Uraemic pericarditis / cardiac tamponade
  - Seizures (uraemic encephalopathy)
- **Chronic:**
  - CV disease (hypertension, LVH, heart failure, accelerated atherosclerosis)
  - Anaemia (iron + EPO deficiency)
  - Renal bone disease (osteodystrophy, fractures)
  - Malnutrition (anorexia, uraemic toxins)
  - Infections (impaired immune function)
  - Electrolyte/acid-base disturbances (metabolic acidosis, hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia)
sections.Prognosis: - Stage-dependent: G3a – most die from CV disease before ESRD; G4–G5 – 10–20% per year progress to dialysis; median survival on dialysis is 5–10 years (UKRR data). Early nephrology referral improves outcomes.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check old creatinine** to distinguish CKD from AKI – ask patient for previous results or check GP letter
- **Calculate eGFR automatically** – do not rely on creatinine alone, especially in elderly/low muscle mass (creatinine may be falsely low)
- **Offer annual flu and pneumococcal vaccine** to all patients with CKD (regardless of stage)
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Not recognising obstruction** as cause of renal impairment – always arrange renal ultrasound within 24 hours of admission
- **Giving NSAIDs** for pain in patients with known or suspected CKD – even single doses can precipitate AKI
- **Not adjusting drug doses for eGFR** – common with antibiotics (e.g., ciprofloxacin, amoxicillin, vancomycin), metformin, digoxin, LMWH – check renal prescribing in BNF
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Breathlessness (new or worsening), orthopnoea, frothy urine
  - Confusion, hiccups, vomiting, muscle twitching (uraemia)
  - Reduced urine output (<400 mL/day or <0.5 mL/kg/h)
  - Blood in urine, or new/progressive skin itching
- **Follow-up:**
  - Newly diagnosed CKD (stage G3–G5): GP with U&E in 2–4 weeks, then routine 3–12 monthly monitoring
  - If eGFR <30 or rapid decline: nephrology clinic within 4 weeks (urgent)
  - After AKI on CKD: repeat U&E in 1–2 weeks
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / nephrology reg if:**
  - K+ >6.5 mmol/L (or >6.0 with ECG changes) – immediately
  - Severe pulmonary oedema not responding to furosemide
  - GCS <13 or new neurological symptoms (uraemic encephalopathy)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising lactate (>2 mmol/L)
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours despite fluid resuscitation
- **ICU / crash team:**
  - Cardiac arrest, respiratory arrest
  - GCS ≤8
  - Refractory hyperkalaemia with arrhythmia
  - Severe acidosis (pH <7.15) with circulatory compromise

rawMarkdown:
## Chronic Kidney Disease
**One-liner:** Progressive loss of kidney function (eGFR <60 mL/min for >3 months) – common, silent, high CV risk.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hyperkalaemia:**
  - Single finding: ECG changes – peaked T waves, loss of P wave, widened QRS (call this "tented T" or "sine wave" – do not wait for K+ result if ECG is abnormal)
- **Pulmonary oedema / fluid overload:**
  - Single finding: Bilateral crackles up to lung apices + orthopnoea + raised JVP – patient cannot lie flat
- **Uraemic pericarditis / tamponade:**
  - Single finding: Chest pain + pericardial rub + hypotension + pulsus paradoxus on examination

### Presentation
- Asymptomatic in early stages (incidental finding on bloods)
- Later: fatigue, pruritus, anorexia, nausea, weight loss, restless legs, peripheral oedema, dyspnoea, confusion, hiccups
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hypertension (often resistant)
  - Pallor, bruising, scratch marks
  - Peripheral oedema (pitting), pleural effusion, ascites
  - Asterixis (flapping tremor) if uraemic
  - Foetor uraemicus (urine-like breath)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute kidney injury (AKI):** acute rise in creatinine (hours–days), often normal or large kidneys on ultrasound, no anaemia / mineral bone disease (unless pre-existing CKD)
- **Pre-renal AKI:** history of vomiting/diarrhoea, dehydration, low urine sodium (<20 mmol/L), rapid response to fluids
- **Obstructive uropathy:** acute or chronic – pain, anuria, palpable bladder; USS shows hydronephrosis
- **Drug-induced renal impairment:** NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, contrast, aminoglycosides – check drug history
- **Decompensated chronic heart failure:** mimics CKD fluid overload but BNP raised, echo shows systolic/diastolic dysfunction
- **Splitter:** Renal ultrasound showing **small, echogenic kidneys** (≤9 cm) strongly favours CKD over AKI; normal or large kidneys with normal echogenicity suggests AKI (or polycystic kidney disease)

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine dipstick – protein, blood, leucocytes, nitrites, glucose
  - ECG – assess for hyperkalaemia (tented T, wide QRS)
  - Fluid balance chart + daily weight
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine, eGFR (using CKD-EPI equation)
  - FBC (normocytic anaemia), bone profile (Ca, PO4, ALP), bicarbonate, albumin
  - PTH, vitamin D (if G4–G5)
  - Serum bicarbonate (assess metabolic acidosis)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Renal ultrasound** (size, echogenicity, cortical thickness, hydronephrosis, stones)
  - Second-line: CT KUB without contrast if obstruction suspected but not confirmed on USS
- **Special tests:**
  - Urine protein:creatinine ratio (PCR) or albumin:creatinine ratio (ACR) – quantify proteinuria (A1: <3, A2: 3–30, A3: >30 mg/mmol)
  - Serology if suspected glomerulonephritis: ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, complement C3/C4, hepatitis B/C, HIV
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **KDIGO / NICE classification:**
    - eGFR G1: ≥90; G2: 60–89; G3a: 45–59; G3b: 30–44; G4: 15–29; G5: <15
    - Combine with ACR category (A1/A2/A3) – higher ACR = worse prognosis
  - Diagnosis requires eGFR <60 or ACR >3 for >3 months (with or without structural damage)
- **Never-miss test:** **Renal ultrasound (or CT) to exclude obstruction** – omission can lead to irreversible renal loss from treatable hydronephrosis

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.5 or >6.0 with ECG changes):**
    - IV calcium gluconate 10 mL 10% over 5–10 min (cardioprotection)
    - IV insulin 10 units Actrapid + 50 mL 50% glucose (shift K+ intracellularly)
    - Consider salbutamol nebulised 5–10 mg (adjunct)
    - Arrange review for dialysis if refractory
  - **Pulmonary oedema:**
    - Sit patient upright, high-flow O2, IV furosemide 40–80 mg (if not anuric)
    - Consider IV nitrates (GTN 10–200 mcg/min) for preload reduction
    - If no response – urgent nephrology review + dialysis (usually haemodialysis)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **Stop nephrotoxic drugs:** NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB temporarily if AKI on CKD, metformin (if eGFR <30), gentamicin (avoid unless essential)
  - **Fluid management:** restrict if oedema (1–1.5 L/day); replace obligate losses if dehydrated
  - **Metabolic acidosis:** if HCO3 <22, consider oral sodium bicarbonate 500 mg–1 g tds [check local policy]
  - **Anaemia:** check iron, folate, B12; start oral iron if ferritin low (ferrous sulphate 200 mg tds); if persistent with eGFR <30, consider ESA (e.g., epoetin alfa – dose per local protocol)
  - **CKD-mineral bone disorder:** check PO4, Ca, PTH; if PO4 >1.5 mmol/L, start phosphate binder (e.g., calcium carbonate 500 mg–1 g with meals); vitamin D – prescribe colecalciferol if deficient or alfacalcidol for active vitamin D [check local policy]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **BP target:** <130/80 mmHg (or <125/75 if ACR >70 mg/mmol) – use ACEi/ARB as first-line (see Drugs)
  - **Diabetes management:** optimise HbA1c <58 mmol/mol; adjust insulin doses if eGFR <30 (reduced clearance)
  - **Lifestyle:** smoking cessation, low-salt diet (<6 g/day), exercise, maintain BMI 20–25
  - **Vaccinations:** seasonal flu, pneumococcal, hepatitis B (if planning RRT)
  - **Referral to nephrology:** eGFR <30 (G4–G5), rapid decline (>5 mL/min/year), ACR >70 mg/mmol, or resistant hypertension
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV fluids only if hypovolaemic – use 0.9% saline (avoid Hartmann’s if K+ high); restrict to 1–1.5 L/day IV/PO if fluid overloaded
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH at standard doses – check anti-Xa if eGFR <30 (use enoxaparin 20 mg daily or tinzaparin 3500 units daily) [check local policy]
  - **Diet:** normal unless fluid overloaded (then 1–1.5 L fluid restriction; if hyperkalaemic – low-K+ diet)
  - **Observation:** 4–6 hourly NEWS, daily standing weight, strict fluid balance, daily U&E

### Drugs & Doses
- **ACEi (ramipril):** start 1.25 mg od, titrate to 2.5–10 mg od – reduce dose if eGFR <30, check K+ and creatinine 1–2 weeks after each dose increase. **ARB (losartan):** 25–50 mg od, same monitoring.
- **Furosemide:** 40–80 mg IV/PO, max 250 mg IV over 4 h (if oliguric). Monitor K+, Mg.
- **Statin (atorvastatin):** 20 mg od for primary CV prevention; 40–80 mg od for secondary (no dose adjustment in CKD)
- **Anaemia:**
  - Oral iron: ferrous sulphate 200 mg tds
  - IV iron: e.g., ferric carboxymaltose 500–1000 mg as per local protocol
  - Epoetin alfa: [check local policy] – typically 2000–4000 IU SC weekly
- **Phosphate binders:** calcium carbonate 500–1000 mg with meals; sevelamer (if hypercalcaemia) 800–1600 mg tds
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **NSAIDs** – absolute contraindication in CKD (increase risk of AKI, worsen proteinuria)
  - **Metformin** – stop if eGFR <30 (risk of lactic acidosis)
  - **Gentamicin** – avoid unless no alternative; use once-daily dosing + monitor levels (trough <1 mg/L)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting/upscaling **ACEi or ARB without checking K+ and creatinine** within 1–2 weeks – high risk of hyperkalaemia or acute decline in eGFR

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hyperkalaemia (life-threatening arrhythmia)
  - Pulmonary oedema
  - Uraemic pericarditis / cardiac tamponade
  - Seizures (uraemic encephalopathy)
- **Chronic:**
  - CV disease (hypertension, LVH, heart failure, accelerated atherosclerosis)
  - Anaemia (iron + EPO deficiency)
  - Renal bone disease (osteodystrophy, fractures)
  - Malnutrition (anorexia, uraemic toxins)
  - Infections (impaired immune function)
  - Electrolyte/acid-base disturbances (metabolic acidosis, hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia)

### Prognosis
- Stage-dependent: G3a – most die from CV disease before ESRD; G4–G5 – 10–20% per year progress to dialysis; median survival on dialysis is 5–10 years (UKRR data). Early nephrology referral improves outcomes.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check old creatinine** to distinguish CKD from AKI – ask patient for previous results or check GP letter
- **Calculate eGFR automatically** – do not rely on creatinine alone, especially in elderly/low muscle mass (creatinine may be falsely low)
- **Offer annual flu and pneumococcal vaccine** to all patients with CKD (regardless of stage)

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Not recognising obstruction** as cause of renal impairment – always arrange renal ultrasound within 24 hours of admission
- **Giving NSAIDs** for pain in patients with known or suspected CKD – even single doses can precipitate AKI
- **Not adjusting drug doses for eGFR** – common with antibiotics (e.g., ciprofloxacin, amoxicillin, vancomycin), metformin, digoxin, LMWH – check renal prescribing in BNF

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Breathlessness (new or worsening), orthopnoea, frothy urine
  - Confusion, hiccups, vomiting, muscle twitching (uraemia)
  - Reduced urine output (<400 mL/day or <0.5 mL/kg/h)
  - Blood in urine, or new/progressive skin itching
- **Follow-up:**
  - Newly diagnosed CKD (stage G3–G5): GP with U&E in 2–4 weeks, then routine 3–12 monthly monitoring
  - If eGFR <30 or rapid decline: nephrology clinic within 4 weeks (urgent)
  - After AKI on CKD: repeat U&E in 1–2 weeks

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / nephrology reg if:**
  - K+ >6.5 mmol/L (or >6.0 with ECG changes) – immediately
  - Severe pulmonary oedema not responding to furosemide
  - GCS <13 or new neurological symptoms (uraemic encephalopathy)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising lactate (>2 mmol/L)
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours despite fluid resuscitation
- **ICU / crash team:**
  - Cardiac arrest, respiratory arrest
  - GCS ≤8
  - Refractory hyperkalaemia with arrhythmia
  - Severe acidosis (pH <7.15) with circulatory compromise
